findings.reasoning: Address collection specified but validation rules missing. Different countries have different address formats (no state in UK, mandatory neighborhood in Brazil).
findings.recommendation: Add requirement: 'Shipping address validation SHALL be country-aware: (1) Postal code format validated per country (US ZIP: ##### or #####-####, UK postcode: complex format). (2) State/Province required for US, Canada, Australia; optional/hidden for other countries. (3) Optional USPS address verification API integration for US addresses (real-time validation). (4) Display validation errors below form fields, disable Checkout until address valid. (5) For unvalidated addresses, display warning: 'Address could not be verified; shipping may be delayed or refused by carrier. Proceed anyway?'.'

findings.finding_title: Missing Completeness: Payment Method Configuration and Admin Restrictions
findings.what_is_missing: REQ101 displays payment methods but do NOT specify admin capability to: (1) Enable/disable payment methods (e.g., PayPal down for maintenance), (2) Restrict by country/order amount/customer role, (3) Set transaction fees, (4) Configure transaction mode (authorize only vs capture), (5) Test credentials vs. production credentials
findings.where_expected: Admin Payment Configuration section
findings.impact: Admins cannot restrict payment methods; disabled payment gateways still shown to customers; no fee structure for international payments; test/prod confusion
findings.reasoning: Customer sees payment methods but admin CRUD operation for payment method management is missing. Incomplete admin feature.
findings.recommendation: Add requirement: 'Admin Payment Methods Configuration page SHALL allow: (1) Enable/disable each payment method (show/hide from checkout). (2) Restrict by country (PayPal unavailable in certain regions). (3) Restrict by order amount range (credit card min $1, max $9999). (4) Restrict by customer role (gift card only for members). (5) Set transaction fee (flat or percentage). (6) Configure transaction mode: 'Authorize only' vs 'Authorize and Capture'. (7) Test credentials separate from production (development mode). (8) Display method-specific settings: API keys, merchant account, webhook URLs. (9) Test connection to gateway (verify credentials work).'

findings.finding_title: Undefined Behavior of 'Search' with Multiple Search Modes
findings.current_usage: REQ005 (basic search with autocomplete), REQ014 (product search), REQ015 (advanced search) describe overlapping but distinct search modes without clear differentiation or integration points
findings.proposed_definition: A multi-mode keyword retrieval system supporting: (1) Basic/Quick Search: keyword input with autocomplete dropdown suggestions and minimal criteria; (2) Advanced Search: expanded form with keyword, category, manufacturer, price range, and description toggle filters; both modes search product names, descriptions, SKUs, tags, and optionally category/manufacturer names, with results supporting filtering, sorting, and pagination.
findings.reasoning: Three separate search requirements exist without defining: (1) when each search mode is accessible (basic in header, advanced via dedicated page/link), (2) whether advanced search subsumes basic search capabilities, (3) how autocomplete results differ from full search results, (4) integration between search modes (can user easily switch between modes?). Requirement descriptions use different terminology: 'keyword input field' (REQ014), 'Advanced Search page' (REQ015), creating ambiguity about UI organization.
findings.recommendation: Define a unified Search Framework glossary entry covering: (1) Search modes and their UI locations, (2) Autocomplete behavior (character threshold, result count, categories displayed), (3) Basic search criteria (keyword only), (4) Advanced search criteria (all filtering options), (5) Result page features (grid/list, sorting, pagination, filter persistence). Reference this entry in all search-related requirements to eliminate redundancy.

findings.finding_title: Ambiguous Definition of 'Product Sorting' Criteria and Behavior
findings.current_usage: REQ018 lists sorting options (Position, Name, Price, Date of creation) but doesn't define what 'Position' means (featured order, bestseller ranking, manual admin order) or whether 'Date of creation' means product creation or price modification date
findings.proposed_definition: Product sorting allows customers to reorder product listing results by multiple criteria: (1) Position: Default order based on admin-configured display order (manual ranking on category/manufacturer edit page); (2) Name: Alphabetical by product name (A-Z or Z-A); (3) Price: By unit price (Low to High or High to Low); (4) Date of Creation: By product creation date (newest first), reflecting when the product was first added to the catalog. Sorting options are individually enabled/disabled by administrators with configurable display order in Catalog settings → Product sorting panel.
findings.reasoning: REQ018 doesn't clarify: (1) What 'Position' represents (manual order, relevance, featured status), (2) Whether sorting options can include variants based on product configuration, (3) How sorting interacts with filtering (e.g., does sorting apply per category or globally), (4) Whether 'Date of creation' is creation date, last modified date, or publication date. The requirement mentions 'AJAX-based product loading' enabling smoother sorting but doesn't define when AJAX is used vs. full page reload.
findings.recommendation: Expand the definition to clarify each sorting criterion's semantics. Define 'Position' as admin-configured manual order stored in the category/manufacturer associations. Specify that sorting options are configurable and individually enable/disable. Clarify that sorting applies within the current result set (category, manufacturer, search) and not globally. Define interaction with filtering (independent operations). Separate AJAX loading behavior into its own requirement or glossary entry.

findings.finding_title: Missing Definition of 'Wishlist Configuration' Options and Behaviors
findings.current_usage: REQ003 states administrators can 'configure maximum wishlist items, whether to show product images, allow out-of-stock items, and whether items move or copy from wishlist to cart' but doesn't define the specific configuration options or their default values
findings.proposed_definition: Wishlist configuration allows administrators to customize wishlist behavior through Settings → Shopping cart settings → Wishlist panel with options: (1) Maximum wishlist items: Set a limit on total items per wishlist (0 = unlimited); (2) Show product images on wishlist: Display product images in wishlist table rows (configurable via 'Show product images' setting); (3) Allow out-of-stock items in wishlist: Permit adding unavailable products to wishlist; (4) Move vs. copy items to cart: Determines whether 'Add to cart' from wishlist removes items from wishlist (move) or retains them (copy); (5) Display wishlist after adding: Redirect to wishlist page or remain on current page after 'Add to wishlist' click; (6) Disable wishlist button per product: Individual product edit option to hide 'Add to wishlist' button.
findings.reasoning: REQ003 lists configuration options but doesn't specify: (1) Default values for each option, (2) Where administrators configure these settings, (3) How configuration changes affect existing wishlists, (4) Whether configuration is global or per-product, (5) Whether wishlist limits are enforced at add-time or post-limit notification. The move vs. copy distinction is important for user expectations but only briefly mentioned.
findings.recommendation: Create a 'Wishlist Configuration Options' glossary entry listing each configurable setting with: (1) Configuration path in admin interface, (2) Data type and valid values (boolean, integer, etc.), (3) Default value, (4) Scope (global or per-product), (5) User-facing behavior when option is enabled/disabled. Reference this entry in wishlist-related requirements (REQ003, REQ027, REQ031, REQ127).

findings.finding_title: Ambiguous Definition of 'Topic' (Content Pages) vs. Other Content Types
findings.current_usage: Requirements refer to 'Topic', 'Topic/Page', and 'Pages' without clear definitions of scope or distinction from other content types (blog posts, news items)
findings.proposed_definition: Topic (also called Page or Content Page in nopCommerce) is a static content block managed in Content management → Topics, used for informational pages distinct from blog posts or news. Topics support: (1) Rich-text HTML content via editor, (2) SEO configuration (meta title, keywords, description, friendly URL), (3) Conditional display (password protection, customer role/store restrictions), (4) Navigation inclusion (top menu, footer columns, sitemap), (5) System topics with fixed names (e.g., 'ConditionsOfUse' for Terms of Service, 'PrivacyPolicy'), (6) Multi-language support. Topics are distinct from Blog Posts (dated, comments, tags) and News Items (dated announcements).
findings.reasoning: Requirements use 'Topic' and 'Topic/Page' inconsistently (REQ010: 'Topics (pages)', REQ049: 'Topic/Page', REQ117: 'Topic/Page'). The definition doesn't clarify: (1) Distinction from other content types, (2) System topics vs. custom topics, (3) System topic naming conventions (how do developers know 'ConditionsOfUse' vs. other names?), (4) Whether Topics can be scheduled with start/end dates like News and Blog posts.
findings.recommendation: Create a 'Topic' glossary entry clarifying the content type's purpose (static informational pages), distinguishing it from Blog Posts (dated, commented, tagged) and News Items (announcements). Define system topics and their purposes (ConditionsOfUse, PrivacyPolicy, etc.). Reference this entry in all Topic-related requirements (REQ010, REQ049, REQ117). If Topics support scheduling, add that to the definition; if not, explicitly state they're always-published.

findings.finding_title: Missing Outcome: Blog Comment Moderation Notifications
findings.outcome_description: The system will notify the blog post author and administrator when new comments are submitted
findings.triggering_scenario: Customer or guest submits a comment on a blog post (F22, REQ150-REQ152)
findings.reasoning: REQ150 mentions 'The store owner is notified of new blog comments if Notify about new blog comments is enabled' but this is stated as a FEATURE CONFIGURATION, not a requirement OUTCOME. There is no explicit requirement that states: 'When a blog comment is submitted, the system WILL send a notification email.' The outcome is implied through configuration but not formally specified. Also missing: what happens when comment is approved? Is the commenter notified?
findings.recommendation: Create explicit requirements: (1) 'When a blog comment is submitted and Blog comments must be approved is enabled, the system SHALL send a moderation notification email to the administrator with comment details and approval/rejection links.' (2) 'When a blog comment is approved, the system SHALL send a notification email to the commenter confirming publication.' These should be standalone requirements, not just configuration descriptions.

findings.finding_title: Null Outcome: Coupon Expiration and Validity Notifications
findings.outcome_description: The system will handle expired coupon codes and notify customers of invalid or expired coupons
findings.triggering_scenario: Customer attempts to apply an expired, invalid, or maximum-usage coupon during checkout
findings.reasoning: REQ043 describes coupon application features: 'start/end date scheduling, cumulative discount support, usage limitations (unlimited, N times only, N times per customer), maximum discounted quantity' but does NOT specify the outcome when validation fails. If a coupon is expired, has reached usage limits, or is invalid, what happens? Is an error message displayed? Is it a clear message? Are these outcomes specified? The requirement describes configuration but not system behavior outcomes when validation fails. REQ047 mentions discounts are displayed but not what happens when a discount becomes invalid.
findings.recommendation: Add requirement: 'When a customer applies a coupon code that is invalid, expired, already used to limit, or does not meet requirements (min order amount, customer role, etc.), the system SHALL display a clear error message indicating the specific reason and next steps. Examples: Coupon has expired as of [date], Coupon usage limit reached for this account, Coupon requires minimum order of $X.' Specify error message requirements.

findings.finding_title: Vague Qualitative Language: 'Appropriate'
findings.text_excerpt: REQ001: 'the system shall support international currency formats'. REQ066: 'validates required fields, email format, zip code format based on country'
findings.interpretation_a: Any format recognized by locale libraries is acceptable
findings.interpretation_b: Only officially recognized/standard formats are acceptable
findings.reasoning: Requirements say 'international formats' but what defines 'appropriate' international format? For currency: EUR 1.234,56 vs €1,234.56? For zip: alphanumeric postal codes? No standards referenced (ISO 4217, etc.). Testing ambiguous.
findings.recommendation: Specify: 'The system SHALL support currency format per ISO 4217 standard. Examples: USD (1,234.56), EUR (1.234,56), GBP (£1,234.56)'

findings.finding_title: Vague Validation: 'Validation Ensures' Without Specifying Errors
findings.text_excerpt: REQ066: 'Both client-side (JavaScript/jQuery validation) and server-side validation are performed. Error messages appear inline next to the relevant field'. REQ090: 'Both client-side (JavaScript) and server-side validation are performed. Validation errors are displayed inline next to the specific field.'
findings.interpretation_a: Validation errors prevent form submission AND are displayed inline
findings.interpretation_b: Errors are displayed inline but form submission might be allowed with warnings
findings.reasoning: Requirements say 'displayed inline' but don't explicitly state if submission is BLOCKED or WARNED. For critical fields like zip code, is submission prevented until corrected? Or does it show a warning allowing override?
findings.recommendation: Specify: 'Server-side validation SHALL prevent form submission if required fields are empty or invalid. An error message SHALL be displayed inline next to the field until corrected and form resubmitted'

findings.finding_title: Vague Scope: 'All Published Categories' Without Clear Hierarchy Rules
findings.text_excerpt: REQ110: 'The sitemap page includes a Categories section listing all published product categories in their hierarchical structure (parent categories with subcategories indented)'.
findings.interpretation_a: 'All published' = show every published category, even hidden ones not in menu
findings.interpretation_b: 'All published' = only show categories marked 'Include in top menu'
findings.reasoning: What if a category is Published but hidden from menu? Does it appear in sitemap? Can admin hide categories from sitemap separately? Scope not defined.
findings.recommendation: Clarify: 'The sitemap SHALL display all categories where Published=true and Include in sitemap=true. Hidden categories (Published=false) SHALL NOT appear'

findings.finding_title: Inconsistency: Tag Cloud Size Calculation Rules Undefined
findings.text_excerpt: REQ023: 'the size of each tag is proportional to the number of products associated with it — more products means a larger tag in the cloud'. REQ151: 'Tags are displayed on the blog page sidebar as a tag cloud where the size of each tag is proportional to the number of posts associated with it'.
findings.interpretation_a: Size = linear relationship (2x products = 2x larger text)
findings.interpretation_b: Size = logarithmic relationship (prevents huge tags from dominatingcloud)
findings.reasoning: Both requirements describe 'proportional' but don't specify the calculation. If one product has 1000 tags and another has 10, should the ratio be 100x font size or logarithmic scaling? Implementation varies.
findings.recommendation: Specify: 'Tag size SHALL be calculated using logarithmic scaling: font_size = min_size + (log(count) / log(max_count)) * (max_size - min_size). Minimum font size: 10px, Maximum font size: 24px'

findings.finding_title: Configuration Settings Mentioned But Not Modeled as Features
findings.business_story: As a store administrator, I want to configure system settings and feature behavior through admin interfaces, So that I can customize the platform without code changes
findings.reasoning: Requirements extensively reference admin configuration (e.g., 'configurable via Configuration → Settings → Catalog settings'). However, there is NO explicit feature describing the store administration/configuration interface. The requirements assume admin capabilities exist but don't define them as features. From a user perspective, 'Store Configuration' is an important admin feature that should be explicitly modeled.
findings.recommendation: Create new features for admin capabilities: 'F31: Store Configuration Interface' with sub-features for each admin area (Catalog Configuration, Order Settings, Payment Configuration, etc.). This makes admin functionality explicit and testable. While less critical for customer-facing features, admin usability significantly impacts operational efficiency.

findings.finding_title: Granularity Inconsistency: Form Fields As Features
findings.business_story: As a requirements analyst, I want consistent granularity across all features so that readers understand what constitutes a 'feature' vs. an 'implementation detail'
findings.reasoning: Requirements REQ117-142 are extremely granular, treating individual form fields as separate requirements (REQ136: 'Name Input', REQ137: 'Email Input', REQ138: 'Enquiry Input'). This is inconsistent with other areas where entire features (e.g., shopping cart with 9+ actions) are grouped together. This granularity makes it hard to understand feature scope — are name/email inputs really separate features or just form fields within a 'Contact Form' feature?
findings.recommendation: Standardize granularity: Features should represent distinct user capabilities/workflows, not form fields. The Contact Form feature (F09a) should be specified at the level of 'Customers can submit inquiries via contact form' with form field specifications in acceptance criteria, not as separate requirements. This makes the feature list easier to navigate and more aligned with user-centric decomposition.

D.current_usage: REQ001 and REQ024 both describe the same functionality (currency selection from dropdown menu) but are listed as separate requirements
D.proposed_definition: A single unified requirement for currency selection should replace both REQ001 and REQ024
D.reasoning: Requirements REQ001 (F01_Currency Selection) and REQ024 (F24_Currency Selection) contain nearly identical descriptions. This redundancy suggests either: (1) accidental duplication during requirements gathering, or (2) unclear business logic regarding whether currency selection appears in multiple locations. The requirements use identical wording: 'The system shall allow users to select their preferred currency from a dropdown menu...'
D.recommendation: Consolidate REQ001 and REQ024 into a single requirement or clearly differentiate their functional scope. If they represent different UI locations (header vs. footer), explicitly state the distinction in the requirement description.

D.finding_title: Ambiguous Definition of 'Continue Button' Across Multiple Steps
D.current_usage: REQ065, REQ074, REQ081, REQ089 all reference 'Continue' buttons but with different contexts (billing → shipping, shipping → payment, payment → order confirmation)
D.proposed_definition: A context-dependent action button present at each checkout step that validates the current step's data and advances the workflow to the next sequential step; button behavior and enabled state are determined by step-specific validation rules
D.reasoning: The term 'Continue Button' appears in 4 separate requirements covering different checkout steps. While the functional intent is consistent, the requirement descriptions don't clearly define whether the button is always labeled 'Continue', whether behavior differs by step, or how validation failures affect button enablement. This ambiguity could lead to inconsistent implementation.
D.recommendation: Create a unified 'Continue Button Behavior' glossary entry specifying: (1) button appears on all checkout steps except confirmation, (2) button is disabled until step-specific validation passes, (3) clicking continues to next step, (4) label remains 'Continue' across all steps (unless explicitly different, e.g., 'Confirm Order'). Reference this entry in all step-specific requirements.

D.finding_title: Undefined Business Logic for 'View Mode' Display Options
D.current_usage: REQ025 mentions both Grid and List view modes for product listings, but doesn't define how the view mode is triggered, persisted, or displayed
D.proposed_definition: A user-selectable presentation format for product listings where Grid view displays products in a multi-column card layout, and List view displays products in a single-column layout with more detailed information; the selected mode persists within the current session.
D.reasoning: REQ025 states 'The system shall provide options to switch between grid and list views for search results' but doesn't define: (1) how users trigger the switch (dropdown, icon buttons, toggle), (2) which format is the default, (3) whether persistence is session-wide or page-specific, (4) what content differences exist between views. REQ006 references 'configurable view modes (grid/list)' but again lacks specifics.
D.recommendation: Expand the definition to specify: (1) UI control type for switching (icon buttons are mentioned in REQ025 as 'grid icon / list icon'), (2) default view mode per page type, (3) persistence scope (session-wide across category pages), (4) content differences (Grid: card layout; List: single column with descriptions), (5) whether view mode selection is global or per-page.

D.finding_title: Inconsistent Definition of 'Required Fields' Validation Across Forms
D.current_usage: REQ062, REQ122, REQ139 all reference mandatory field enforcement and asterisk (*) marking, but requirements also state that 'All enabled fields... can be individually configured as required' (REQ062), creating ambiguity about which fields are always required vs. configurable
D.proposed_definition: Form input fields marked with a red asterisk (*) indicator that must contain valid data before form submission; which fields are required is configurable by administrators but typically include critical fields (First name, Last name, Email, Address 1, City, Country, Postal code); the system enforces required field validation both client-side (JavaScript, immediate feedback) and server-side (security).
D.reasoning: Multiple requirements mention mandatory field enforcement but don't clearly state: (1) which fields are required by default vs. configurable, (2) whether some fields are always required regardless of configuration, (3) what constitutes a 'critical field' vs. 'optional field'. This is especially problematic for address forms where requirements state both 'Default mandatory fields typically include...' (REQ062) and 'each field... can be individually enabled/disabled and set as required/optional' (REQ061), suggesting inconsistent requirements logic.
D.recommendation: Define a standard set of 'always required' fields (e.g., First name, Last name, Email, Country, City, Address, Postal code) vs. 'conditionally required' fields (configurable by admin). Clarify that the asterisk mark indicates dynamic requirement status that may vary by configuration. Create a single 'Form Field Requirements Framework' entry to be referenced by all form-related requirements.

D.current_usage: REQ042 mentions checkout attributes for gift wrapping with price adjustments and conditional display, but the glossary entry for 'Checkout Attributes' is overly generic and doesn't specify implementation mechanics
D.proposed_definition: Dynamic, configurable form fields presented at specific points during checkout (shopping cart page, confirmed order page) that apply to the entire order or individual products. Checkout attributes support: control types (dropdown, radio buttons, checkboxes, color selector), optional price/weight adjustments per value, tax categories, conditional display rules (e.g., 'Shippable product required'), and required/optional designation.
D.reasoning: REQ042 describes gift wrapping as a checkout attribute but introduces terms like 'control type', 'price adjustments', 'weight adjustments', 'tax category', and 'Shippable product required' without prior definition. The requirement doesn't explain: (1) when checkout attributes are collected relative to other checkout steps, (2) how multiple checkout attributes are ordered/displayed, (3) whether checkout attributes affect shipping cost calculations, (4) how they differ from product attributes.
D.recommendation: Create a comprehensive glossary entry defining checkout attributes with: (1) collection point(s) in checkout flow (shopping cart page), (2) potential control types and how they're rendered, (3) price/weight adjustment calculation mechanics, (4) conditional display rule syntax/examples, (5) validation requirements. Clarify distinction between checkout attributes (order-level) and product attributes (product-level).

D.finding_title: Conflicting Definition of 'Add to Cart' Behavior from Product Variants
D.current_usage: REQ017 states 'For products with required attributes... the user is redirected to the product details page' while REQ029 states 'Before adding, the user can select product attributes' on the product details page itself
D.proposed_definition: The 'Add to cart' action's behavior depends on product configuration: (1) Simple products or products without required attributes: add directly with default quantity 1; (2) Products with required attributes on listing pages: redirect to product details page for attribute selection; (3) Products with optional attributes on listing pages: add with default quantity 1, optionally redirecting for customization; (4) On product details page: allow attribute selection before adding.
D.reasoning: REQ017 clearly states redirection for required attributes, but the definition doesn't account for optional vs. required attributes distinction. REQ029 assumes the user is already on the product details page. This creates ambiguity: What happens when a user clicks 'Add to cart' on a listing page for a product with optional (not required) attributes? Are they redirected, or added with defaults?
D.recommendation: Revise the definition to explicitly address: (1) Required attribute scenario: always redirect to product details page on listing page 'Add to cart' clicks, (2) Optional attribute scenario: add with defaults on listing pages, optionally offering a redirect link, (3) Product details page scenario: allow full attribute selection before adding. Clarify distinction between required vs. optional attributes in the Product Attributes glossary entry.

D.finding_title: Missing Definition of 'Back Button' Scope and Data Preservation
D.current_usage: REQ073, REQ080, REQ088, REQ094 all reference Back buttons enabling backward navigation in checkout, but don't specify whether all previously entered data is preserved across multiple back-forth cycles
D.proposed_definition: A navigation button present on each checkout step (except the first step: Billing Address) that returns the customer to the previous step while preserving all previously entered data (addresses, shipping selection, payment method) within the current session. Data persists through multiple back-forth navigation cycles until checkout completion or session termination.
D.reasoning: While requirements state 'preserving all previously entered data', they don't clarify: (1) data scope (all form fields, selected options, etc.), (2) preservation duration (session only, or persistent across sessions), (3) behavior if data validation rules change between back-forth navigation, (4) handling of address deletions/modifications made after back navigation. REQ073 says 'while preserving all previously entered data' but REQ088 says 'preserving entered credit card data in the current session', introducing session-specific language.
D.recommendation: Standardize the Back button definition across all checkout step requirements to consistently state: (1) Button appears on steps 2+ only, (2) Clicking Back navigates to the previous step, (3) All step-specific data is preserved in session memory, (4) Data persists through unlimited back-forth navigation, (5) Data is cleared only upon successful order completion or explicit session termination/timeout. Avoid session-specific language in some requirements but not others.

D.finding_title: Undefined Behavior of 'Search' with Multiple Search Modes
D.current_usage: REQ005 (basic search with autocomplete), REQ014 (product search), REQ015 (advanced search) describe overlapping but distinct search modes without clear differentiation or integration points
D.proposed_definition: A multi-mode keyword retrieval system supporting: (1) Basic/Quick Search: keyword input with autocomplete dropdown suggestions and minimal criteria; (2) Advanced Search: expanded form with keyword, category, manufacturer, price range, and description toggle filters; both modes search product names, descriptions, SKUs, tags, and optionally category/manufacturer names, with results supporting filtering, sorting, and pagination.
D.reasoning: Three separate search requirements exist without defining: (1) when each search mode is accessible (basic in header, advanced via dedicated page/link), (2) whether advanced search subsumes basic search capabilities, (3) how autocomplete results differ from full search results, (4) integration between search modes (can user easily switch between modes?). Requirement descriptions use different terminology: 'keyword input field' (REQ014), 'Advanced Search page' (REQ015), creating ambiguity about UI organization.
D.recommendation: Define a unified Search Framework glossary entry covering: (1) Search modes and their UI locations, (2) Autocomplete behavior (character threshold, result count, categories displayed), (3) Basic search criteria (keyword only), (4) Advanced search criteria (all filtering options), (5) Result page features (grid/list, sorting, pagination, filter persistence). Reference this entry in all search-related requirements to eliminate redundancy.

D.term: Credit Card Data Storage and Processing
D.current_usage: REQ085 states 'the full card number should only be transmitted over HTTPS/SSL encrypted connections' while REQ091 states 'by default, credit card details are not stored in the nopCommerce database (except when using the Manual Credit Card method, where encrypted storage is optional)'
D.proposed_definition: Credit card data handling follows PCI DSS compliance: (1) For most payment methods (PayPal, Stripe, Square, Authorize.Net): Card data is transmitted to payment gateway via HTTPS, never stored in nopCommerce database; (2) For Manual Credit Card method: Card data is optionally stored in encrypted form in nopCommerce database if configured; (3) In all cases, full card numbers are never logged or transmitted insecurely; CVV is never stored; tokenization is preferred over storage; access to stored card data is restricted and audited.
D.reasoning: REQ085 emphasizes transmission security but doesn't address storage. REQ091 clarifies storage exceptions but doesn't fully explain payment gateway delegation. The two requirements create potential confusion: Does 'Manual Credit Card method' mean customers enter cards in nopCommerce forms (risky), or does it mean card info is processed manually by the merchant (after being transmitted securely)? The phrase 'encrypted storage is optional' could be misinterpreted as suggesting unencrypted storage is acceptable.
D.recommendation: Create a comprehensive 'Payment Card Data Security Policy' glossary entry covering: (1) Payment method categories and data flows for each, (2) Encryption requirements for transmission and optional storage, (3) PCI DSS compliance obligations, (4) What data is stored where (gateway vs. local database), (5) Token-based payment recommendations, (6) CVV handling (never stored). Revise REQ085 and REQ091 to reference this entry and clarify the distinction between payment methods.

D.finding_title: Ambiguous Definition of 'Responsive Design' and Device Support
D.current_usage: REQ116 states 'automatically adapts to different screen sizes — desktop monitors, tablets, and mobile phones' but doesn't define what 'adapts' means (layout reflow, feature hiding, functionality preservation)
D.proposed_definition: Responsive design is a web design approach where the user interface layout, content arrangement, and interactive elements dynamically adjust based on the viewport size and device type. Adaptations include: flexible grid layouts that reflow columns, CSS media queries for breakpoint-specific styling, relative sizing (percentages vs. fixed pixels), touch-friendly spacing for mobile interactions, and hamburger menus for mobile navigation. Core functionality and content remain accessible on all supported devices (desktop, tablet, mobile).
D.reasoning: REQ116 describes responsive design symptoms but not mechanics. Stakeholders might interpret 'adapts' differently: Does hamburger menu replacement count as adaptation? Do features get hidden on mobile (reducing functionality)? Is the site mobile-first or desktop-first? The requirement lists 'Supported browsers: Microsoft Edge, Mozilla Firefox, Google Chrome, and Apple Safari' but doesn't address mobile browser support (e.g., Chrome Mobile, Safari Mobile). The term 'responsive design' is used without defining minimum/maximum supported viewport sizes or breakpoints.
D.recommendation: Expand the definition to specify: (1) Supported viewport ranges (e.g., 320px mobile to 2560px desktop), (2) Breakpoints and layout changes (e.g., 768px for tablet, 1024px for desktop), (3) Mobile-specific UI components (hamburger menu, touch-friendly buttons), (4) Content prioritization strategy (what's prioritized on mobile vs. desktop), (5) Supported device types and browsers including mobile variants, (6) Accessibility requirements across all viewport sizes. Reference this definition in other responsive design requirements.

D.finding_title: Undefined Scope of 'Order Confirmation Page' Content and Behavior
D.current_usage: REQ092 references 'Confirm Order step' (checkout workflow), REQ099 references 'Order Completed page', and REQ100 references 'Order Details page', creating ambiguity about whether these are the same page or different pages
D.proposed_definition: Multiple order-related pages exist in distinct contexts: (1) Confirm Order step: Final checkout step before order placement, displays complete order summary with all details and 'Confirm' button; (2) Order Completed page: Redirect page shown immediately after successful order placement, displays order summary, number, date, total; (3) Order Details page: Accessed via My Account → Orders list, displays comprehensive order information with additional actions (print, PDF, reorder), accessible anytime after checkout.
D.reasoning: The three terms reference overlapping functionality but don't clarify relationships. REQ099 states 'After successful order placement, the Order Completed page (or Order Details page)' using 'or', suggesting they might be the same page with a 'Disable Order completed page' option redirecting to Order Details. However, subsequent requirements treat Order Details page as a separate, persistent page. Users unfamiliar with nopCommerce architecture might not understand: (1) When each page is shown, (2) What content differs between pages, (3) Whether the Order Completed page is a temporary redirect or persistent page.
D.recommendation: Create three distinct glossary entries: (1) 'Confirm Order Step': Last checkout step before order submission, (2) 'Order Completed Page': Post-checkout confirmation page (potentially disabled via configuration), (3) 'Order Details Page': Persistent page accessible via customer account. Clarify in requirements the 'Disable Order completed page' configuration's effect (redirects directly to Order Details page). Define what content is displayed on each page and how they interrelate.

D.finding_title: Missing Definition of 'Terms of Service Agreement' Compliance Flow
D.current_usage: REQ049 states the requirement can appear on 'shopping cart page' or 'confirm order page' but doesn't clarify the enforcement logic when both options are configured
D.proposed_definition: A mandatory checkbox agreement requiring customers to accept the store's Terms of Service before proceeding with purchase. The agreement can be enforced at one of two checkout points: (1) Shopping Cart Page: Checkbox appears on the shopping cart; checkout button is disabled until checked; (2) Confirm Order Page: Checkbox appears on the final order confirmation step before 'Confirm' button. Only one enforcement point is active per configuration. The Terms of Service content is managed as a Topic with system name 'ConditionsOfUse' and displayed in a popup/modal when the checkbox link is clicked.
D.reasoning: REQ049 states 'Two options are available' suggesting mutual exclusivity, but doesn't explicitly state that only one can be active. The requirement doesn't clarify: (1) What happens if both are enabled (conflict resolution), (2) Whether the requirement can appear in multiple locations with different content, (3) Whether users must agree at both steps if somehow both are enabled, (4) How the Terms of Service content is versioned if updated mid-customer-journey.
D.recommendation: Clarify that Terms of Service agreement enforcement is mutually exclusive: only one location (shopping cart or confirm order) can be active. Define default configuration. Specify that the Topic content ('ConditionsOfUse') is version-controlled and customers must agree to the version displayed at the time of checkout. If future requirements need agreement at multiple steps, revise this definition accordingly.

D.finding_title: Ambiguous Definition of 'Product Sorting' Criteria and Behavior
D.current_usage: REQ018 lists sorting options (Position, Name, Price, Date of creation) but doesn't define what 'Position' means (featured order, bestseller ranking, manual admin order) or whether 'Date of creation' means product creation or price modification date
D.proposed_definition: Product sorting allows customers to reorder product listing results by multiple criteria: (1) Position: Default order based on admin-configured display order (manual ranking on category/manufacturer edit page); (2) Name: Alphabetical by product name (A-Z or Z-A); (3) Price: By unit price (Low to High or High to Low); (4) Date of Creation: By product creation date (newest first), reflecting when the product was first added to the catalog. Sorting options are individually enabled/disabled by administrators with configurable display order in Catalog settings → Product sorting panel.
D.reasoning: REQ018 doesn't clarify: (1) What 'Position' represents (manual order, relevance, featured status), (2) Whether sorting options can include variants based on product configuration, (3) How sorting interacts with filtering (e.g., does sorting apply per category or globally), (4) Whether 'Date of creation' is creation date, last modified date, or publication date. The requirement mentions 'AJAX-based product loading' enabling smoother sorting but doesn't define when AJAX is used vs. full page reload.
D.recommendation: Expand the definition to clarify each sorting criterion's semantics. Define 'Position' as admin-configured manual order stored in the category/manufacturer associations. Specify that sorting options are configurable and individually enable/disable. Clarify that sorting applies within the current result set (category, manufacturer, search) and not globally. Define interaction with filtering (independent operations). Separate AJAX loading behavior into its own requirement or glossary entry.

D.finding_title: Conflicting Guidance on 'Email Validation' and Account Activation
D.current_usage: REQ051 states 'Email validation required (confirmation email sent)' and REQ054 states 'After clicking the reset link, the customer is directed to a page' but doesn't clarify whether registration is blocked until email is validated
D.proposed_definition: Email validation registration method requires customers to verify their email address before account activation. Process: (1) Customer submits registration form with email address; (2) System sends a confirmation email to the provided address with a unique, time-limited activation link; (3) Customer clicks the link in the email to verify ownership; (4) Account is activated and customer can log in. During the verification period (before clicking the link), the account status is 'Pending - Email Validation' and login is blocked. The email validity period is configurable (e.g., 24-48 hours).
D.reasoning: REQ051 mentions email validation as a registration method but doesn't clarify: (1) Account status during validation (can user log in immediately?), (2) What happens if the email is invalid (bounce handling), (3) Whether customers can resend the confirmation email, (4) What happens to unverified accounts after expiration (deleted, disabled, or resendable), (5) Interaction with 'Email validation required' vs. 'Admin approval required' (can both be enabled?). REQ054 (Forgot Password) references password reset links with configurable validity but doesn't clarify whether the same mechanism is used for registration email validation.
D.recommendation: Define email validation registration as blocking login until verification. Specify: (1) Link validity period and extension mechanism (resend option), (2) Account status during pending verification, (3) Behavior for expired/invalid emails, (4) Error messages for each scenario, (5) Whether email validation and admin approval are independent or mutually exclusive. If both can be enabled, clarify the order of requirements (validation before approval, or approval before validation).

D.finding_title: Undefined Behavior of 'Estimate Shipping' Across Multiple Checkout Points
D.current_usage: REQ037 mentions shipping estimation on product details page, REQ046 mentions it on shopping cart page, but requirements don't clarify whether estimates are available before/after address entry or how they affect the checkout flow
D.proposed_definition: Estimate Shipping is a feature allowing customers to view available shipping methods and costs without committing to an address or entering full checkout. Available at two points: (1) Product Details Page: 'Estimate shipping' popup allows entering country/state/zip to see shipping options for that specific product; (2) Shopping Cart Page: 'Estimate Shipping' button opens popup for current cart contents, allowing shipping preview. Both popups display available methods, descriptions, and costs. Estimates update order totals but don't lock in the shipping selection until the shipping method step in checkout. Address information entered in estimate popups may pre-populate checkout address fields.
D.reasoning: Requirements state shipping estimation is available on both product details and cart pages but don't clarify: (1) Whether addresses entered in estimates are remembered, (2) How estimates interact with saved customer addresses, (3) Whether estimates affect cart contents (can they view shipping before fully populating the cart?), (4) Whether estimated costs are guaranteed or can change during checkout if address changes, (5) Whether the estimate addresses must be provided or if default store address is used.
D.recommendation: Create a unified 'Shipping Estimation Framework' glossary entry covering: (1) Availability points (product page, cart page, both), (2) Data entry requirements (country/state/zip at minimum, no full address required), (3) Output information (methods, costs, delivery times), (4) Persistence of estimate data (session-only), (5) Pre-population of checkout fields using estimate address, (6) Cost guarantees (estimates may change if address changes in checkout). Reference this entry in product details and cart-related requirements.

D.current_usage: Requirements refer to 'customer role' (REQ006, REQ051, REQ070) and 'customer group' (REQ043) interchangeably without clear definition of whether these are synonymous or different concepts
D.proposed_definition: Customer Role (also referred to as Customer Group in some contexts) is a classification assigned to a customer that determines access permissions and business rule application. Roles include: (1) Guests/Anonymous: Unregistered users viewing the storefront; (2) Registered: Users with accounts, can access wishlists, order history, reward points; (3) Administrators: Store staff with full system access. Roles control visibility of products/categories (ACL restrictions), discount eligibility, forum permissions, and feature availability.
D.reasoning: Requirements use 'customer role' and 'customer group' inconsistently. REQ051 states 'After registration, the customer is assigned to the 'Registered' customer role' while REQ043 references discount features 'assigned to a customer role'. The terms appear synonymous in usage, but nopCommerce documentation sometimes distinguishes 'roles' (permission-based) from 'groups' (discount/business rule groupings). This inconsistency could confuse developers implementing ACL or discount logic.
D.recommendation: Standardize terminology: choose either 'Customer Role' or 'Customer Group' and use consistently throughout all requirements. If they represent different concepts in the actual system, provide explicit definitions distinguishing their purposes and technical implementation. If synonymous, create a single glossary entry and enforce consistent terminology across all requirements.

D.finding_title: Missing Definition of 'Wishlist Configuration' Options and Behaviors
D.current_usage: REQ003 states administrators can 'configure maximum wishlist items, whether to show product images, allow out-of-stock items, and whether items move or copy from wishlist to cart' but doesn't define the specific configuration options or their default values
D.proposed_definition: Wishlist configuration allows administrators to customize wishlist behavior through Settings → Shopping cart settings → Wishlist panel with options: (1) Maximum wishlist items: Set a limit on total items per wishlist (0 = unlimited); (2) Show product images on wishlist: Display product images in wishlist table rows (configurable via 'Show product images' setting); (3) Allow out-of-stock items in wishlist: Permit adding unavailable products to wishlist; (4) Move vs. copy items to cart: Determines whether 'Add to cart' from wishlist removes items from wishlist (move) or retains them (copy); (5) Display wishlist after adding: Redirect to wishlist page or remain on current page after 'Add to wishlist' click; (6) Disable wishlist button per product: Individual product edit option to hide 'Add to wishlist' button.
D.reasoning: REQ003 lists configuration options but doesn't specify: (1) Default values for each option, (2) Where administrators configure these settings, (3) How configuration changes affect existing wishlists, (4) Whether configuration is global or per-product, (5) Whether wishlist limits are enforced at add-time or post-limit notification. The move vs. copy distinction is important for user expectations but only briefly mentioned.
D.recommendation: Create a 'Wishlist Configuration Options' glossary entry listing each configurable setting with: (1) Configuration path in admin interface, (2) Data type and valid values (boolean, integer, etc.), (3) Default value, (4) Scope (global or per-product), (5) User-facing behavior when option is enabled/disabled. Reference this entry in wishlist-related requirements (REQ003, REQ027, REQ031, REQ127).

D.finding_title: Ambiguous Definition of 'Topic' (Content Pages) vs. Other Content Types
D.current_usage: Requirements refer to 'Topic', 'Topic/Page', and 'Pages' without clear definitions of scope or distinction from other content types (blog posts, news items)
D.proposed_definition: Topic (also called Page or Content Page in nopCommerce) is a static content block managed in Content management → Topics, used for informational pages distinct from blog posts or news. Topics support: (1) Rich-text HTML content via editor, (2) SEO configuration (meta title, keywords, description, friendly URL), (3) Conditional display (password protection, customer role/store restrictions), (4) Navigation inclusion (top menu, footer columns, sitemap), (5) System topics with fixed names (e.g., 'ConditionsOfUse' for Terms of Service, 'PrivacyPolicy'), (6) Multi-language support. Topics are distinct from Blog Posts (dated, comments, tags) and News Items (dated announcements).
D.reasoning: Requirements use 'Topic' and 'Topic/Page' inconsistently (REQ010: 'Topics (pages)', REQ049: 'Topic/Page', REQ117: 'Topic/Page'). The definition doesn't clarify: (1) Distinction from other content types, (2) System topics vs. custom topics, (3) System topic naming conventions (how do developers know 'ConditionsOfUse' vs. other names?), (4) Whether Topics can be scheduled with start/end dates like News and Blog posts.
D.recommendation: Create a 'Topic' glossary entry clarifying the content type's purpose (static informational pages), distinguishing it from Blog Posts (dated, commented, tagged) and News Items (announcements). Define system topics and their purposes (ConditionsOfUse, PrivacyPolicy, etc.). Reference this entry in all Topic-related requirements (REQ010, REQ049, REQ117). If Topics support scheduling, add that to the definition; if not, explicitly state they're always-published.

F.business_story: As a customer, I want to select my preferred currency and see all prices converted, So that I understand costs in my local monetary system
F.reasoning: REQ001 (F01_Currency Selection) and REQ024 (F24_Currency Selection) are functionally identical. Both describe the same capability: users selecting currencies from a dropdown, with automatic price recalculation and display in the selected currency using configured exchange rates. This duplication indicates either a requirements gathering error or intentional re-emphasis of an important feature.
F.recommendation: Consolidate into a single requirement. If the feature is critical to the business case, clearly articulate why it appears twice (e.g., 'critical for international sales'). Update requirements numbering to eliminate the duplicate.

F.business_story: As a system architect, I want a clear decomposition of authentication features by granularity level, So that development teams can prioritize independently
F.reasoning: REQ002 bundles multiple authentication capabilities into a single 'User Authentication' feature: (1) Registration with multiple methods, (2) Login with security features, (3) Account management with multiple sub-features (addresses, order history, downloadable products, etc.). This is too coarse for implementation planning and testing. Additionally, other requirements (REQ026, REQ052-057) re-specify login components without clear separation from core authentication.
F.recommendation: Decompose F02 into granular features: F02a (Registration Flow), F02b (Login and Session Management), F02c (Account Profile Management), F02d (Security Enforcement). Each should have its own business story and acceptance criteria. Consolidate scattered login requirements (REQ052-057) under F02b.

F.finding_title: Search Functionality Fragments Across Multiple Requirements
F.business_story: As a product manager, I want a cohesive search feature definition with clear separation of basic vs. advanced capabilities, So that stakeholders understand the complete search offering
F.reasoning: Search is described across multiple scattered requirements: REQ005 (basic search with autocomplete), REQ014 (product search), REQ015 (advanced search), REQ016 (search results display), REQ018 (sorting), REQ019 (results per page). Each requirement adds incrementally to the feature without clear logical separation. The requirements don't explicitly distinguish between 'required for MVP' vs. 'nice-to-have' search capabilities.
F.recommendation: Consolidate under F05 with clear sub-features: (1) Core Search (REQ005, REQ014), (2) Advanced Search (REQ015), (3) Results Management (REQ016, REQ018, REQ019). Each sub-feature should have acceptance criteria for what constitutes 'done'. This supports phased implementation (MVP with core, Phase 2 with advanced).

F.finding_title: Checkout Process Requirements Lack Clear Step Sequencing
F.business_story: As a developer, I want to understand the exact sequence and dependencies of checkout steps, So that I can implement the checkout flow correctly with proper data flow between steps
F.reasoning: Checkout features span REQ037-098 with individual requirements for each step (address REQ061-069, shipping REQ070-076, payment REQ077-091, confirmation REQ092-098). While the individual steps are well-documented, the requirements lack: (1) explicit ordering (which step follows which), (2) data dependency mapping (what data from one step is used in the next), (3) branch conditions (e.g., when is shipping address skipped), (4) validation sequencing. The requirements assume readers understand 'standard' checkout flow but don't articulate it.
F.recommendation: Create a Checkout Flow Diagram showing step sequence, data flow, and branch conditions. Explicitly define: (1) Standard flow: Address → Shipping → Payment → Confirmation, (2) Conditional branches: 'Ship to same address' skips shipping address step, (3) Guest checkout branch: No account creation/login required, (4) For each step, list required input data and output/state changes. Consider creating a separate 'Checkout Flow Architecture' requirement document.

F.finding_title: Product Display Information Is Fragmented Across Multiple Requirements
F.business_story: As a product manager, I want a unified product information feature that clearly defines what content appears on product detail pages, So that the development team implements a consistent user experience
F.reasoning: Product detail page content is scattered across REQ009 (Learn More button, product details), REQ028 (Product Details Display), REQ030 (Quantity Selection), REQ031 (Add to Wishlist), REQ032 (Compare), REQ033 (Email), REQ034 (Breadcrumbs), REQ035 (Reviews). Each requirement adds incrementally to the product page but there's no single unified feature definition showing the complete 'Product Details Page' composition. Designers need a holistic view of all elements that appear together on this page.
F.recommendation: Create a single 'Product Details Page' feature that lists all page sections: (1) Product Identity (name, SKU, breadcrumb), (2) Media (images, videos), (3) Description (short & full), (4) Pricing & Availability, (5) Attributes & Variants, (6) Add to Cart Action, (7) Add to Wishlist, (8) Comparison, (9) Email, (10) Reviews & Ratings, (11) Related/Cross-sell. Each section should have a corresponding sub-feature with requirements. This enables UI/UX designers to create coherent wireframes.

F.finding_title: Shopping Cart Feature Has Multiple Interconnected Capabilities
F.business_story: As a development lead, I want each cart capability separated into independent features with clear implementation boundaries, So that team members can work on different cart features in parallel without blocking each other
F.reasoning: F04 (Shopping Cart) bundles many independent capabilities: add to cart, view cart, update quantities, remove items, apply coupons, apply gift cards, gift wrapping, continue shopping, estimate shipping. While related, these are logically separable and could be tested/released independently. For example, a store could have 'add to cart' without 'apply coupon' initially. The current bundling makes it hard to estimate work or prioritize implementation.
F.recommendation: Decompose F04 into: F04a (Core Cart - add/view/update/remove), F04b (Discount Management - coupons and gift cards), F04c (Shipping Estimation), F04d (Gift Wrapping). Each has independent business value and implementation complexity. This enables: (1) MVP with F04a only, (2) Phase 2 adding F04b, (3) Phase 3 adding F04c/F04d. Maintains coherence through clear dependencies.

F.finding_title: Login-Related Requirements Scattered Across Multiple Features
F.business_story: As a QA lead, I want all login-related requirements consolidated into a single feature specification, So that I can create comprehensive test cases covering all login scenarios
F.reasoning: Login functionality is described in REQ002 (as part of authentication), REQ052 (Login Functionality), REQ053 (Remember Me), REQ054 (Forgot Password), REQ057 (Login Error Messages), REQ060 (Login Button during checkout). These requirements are scattered across different requirement IDs and don't form a cohesive 'Login Feature' specification. A tester reading REQ052 alone might miss Remember Me or error handling requirements.
F.recommendation: Create a consolidated 'Login Feature' specification containing: (1) Login form requirements (email/password fields, validation), (2) Remember Me checkbox behavior, (3) Password recovery flow, (4) Error message handling, (5) Security measures (brute-force protection, lockout), (6) Login success behavior. All requirements (REQ052-054, REQ057) should be consolidated under this single feature with cross-references showing where they appear in different contexts (standard login vs. checkout).

F.finding_title: Contact Form and Customer Support Features Need Consolidation
F.business_story: As a support manager, I want a clear distinction between different customer support channels and their capabilities, So that we implement a cohesive support strategy across all touchpoints
F.reasoning: Customer support capabilities are mentioned across REQ010 (documentation), REQ011 (forums), REQ013 (contact form), REQ133-140 (contact form details). The contact form details (REQ133-140) are extremely granular (name input field, email input field, enquiry input field, etc.) compared to higher-level forum/documentation requirements. This inconsistent granularity makes it hard to see the forest from the trees. Are name/email/enquiry input fields really separate 'features' or just implementation details of 'contact form'?
F.recommendation: Restructure support features hierarchically: F09 (Customer Support) → F09a (Contact Form), F09b (Forum), F09c (Documentation). Only F09a should detail specific form fields (they're implementation, not features). Consolidate REQ133-140 into F09a with a single 'Contact Form' requirement rather than individual field requirements. This maintains appropriate abstraction levels across feature definitions.

F.business_story: As a customer, I want to initiate a return/exchange of a purchased product and track the return status, So that I can resolve issues with my purchases
F.reasoning: REQ002 mentions 'return requests' as part of 'My Account' page ('view... return requests'), but there is NO requirement describing the complete return management feature. The requirements don't cover: (1) How customers initiate returns, (2) Return status tracking, (3) Refund processing, (4) Return shipping, (5) Return approval workflow. REQ002 only implies returns exist as a data display element without specifying the complete feature behavior. This is a critical gap for e-commerce platforms.
F.recommendation: Create a new feature 'F23: Order Return Management' with business story and sub-features: (1) Return Initiation (customer submits return request with reason), (2) Return Approval (admin approves/denies), (3) Return Shipping (generate shipping label, track return), (4) Refund Processing, (5) Return Status Tracking. Add corresponding requirements for each sub-feature. This is critical for customer satisfaction in e-commerce.

F.finding_title: Missing Feature: Inventory and Stock Management Visibility
F.business_story: As a customer, I want to see if a product is in stock, out of stock, or available for pre-order, So that I know whether I can purchase it immediately or if there will be a delay
F.reasoning: Requirements mention 'stock availability' and 'inventory tracking' in the context of validation (REQ029: 'The system validates... stock availability') and 'availability status' display (REQ028: 'availability status'), but there is NO dedicated requirement specifying HOW stock information is displayed to customers. The requirements don't clarify: (1) Where is 'In Stock' / 'Out of Stock' text displayed, (2) Are pre-order and backorder statuses visible, (3) How is stock count displayed (exact number vs. 'Limited stock'), (4) Is there a 'back-in-stock notification' feature (REQ002 mentions it in account features)? This represents a gap in customer-facing inventory visibility.
F.recommendation: Create a new feature 'F26: Stock Availability Display' with sub-features: (1) In-Stock Status Display, (2) Out-of-Stock Handling, (3) Pre-Order Indication, (4) Back-in-Stock Notification. Clearly specify where and how stock information appears on product pages, search results, and catalog listings. This enhances user experience and reduces support inquiries about availability.

F.finding_title: Missing Feature: Promotional Pricing and Discounts Visibility
F.business_story: As a customer, I want to clearly see product discounts, sale prices, and promotional offers on product listings and details, So that I can identify good deals and save money
F.reasoning: Requirements mention 'old price for comparison (strikethrough)' (REQ008, REQ016) and discount code application (REQ043), but there is NO comprehensive feature for displaying promotional pricing and visual indicators (e.g., 'Sale' badge, percent-off labels, countdown timers for limited-time promotions). The requirements show WHAT prices are displayed but not HOW promotional pricing is visually highlighted or communicated to drive conversion. This is a critical gap for e-commerce user experience.
F.recommendation: Create a new feature 'F27: Promotional Pricing Display' with sub-features: (1) Sale Price Highlighting (old price strikethrough, new price emphasis), (2) Discount Percentage/Amount Display, (3) 'Sale' or 'Limited Time' Badges, (4) Promotional Message Display, (5) Countdown Timer for Time-Limited Offers. This clarifies how the store communicates promotional value to customers and improves conversion optimization.

F.finding_title: Missing Feature: Multi-Store and Store Administration
F.business_story: As a customer, I want to understand which store I'm shopping from and (if applicable) switch between multiple branded stores, So that I can access different product catalogs or store configurations
F.reasoning: Requirements frequently mention 'multi-store' context and store-specific visibility settings (e.g., 'limited to specific stores', 'multi-store configurations'), but there is NO customer-facing feature described for: (1) How customers see/switch between stores, (2) Store selection UI, (3) Store-specific catalog/pricing, (4) Store locator feature. The requirements mention multi-store as an admin configuration concern but don't address the customer user experience when multiple stores exist.
F.recommendation: Create a new feature 'F28: Multi-Store Visibility and Switching' with sub-features: (1) Store Selector/Switcher UI, (2) Store-Specific Catalog Display, (3) Store Locator Map, (4) Store-Specific Pricing/Currency Display. This is particularly important if the e-commerce platform serves multiple brands, regions, or languages. Without this feature clarity, developers may implement inconsistent multi-store UX.

F.business_story: As a customer, I want to be notified when an out-of-stock product becomes available again, So that I can quickly purchase it before it sells out
F.reasoning: REQ002 explicitly mentions 'back-in-stock subscriptions' as part of the 'My Account' page, and REQ003 mentions 'allow out-of-stock items' in wishlist configuration. This implies a feature exists for customers to subscribe to stock notifications, but there is NO dedicated requirement describing: (1) How customers subscribe to back-in-stock notifications, (2) Notification trigger conditions, (3) Notification delivery method (email), (4) Subscription management. The feature is implied but not explicitly defined.
F.recommendation: Create a new feature 'F29: Product Availability Notifications' with sub-features: (1) Back-in-Stock Subscription (UI to subscribe), (2) Notification Triggering (when stock changes), (3) Notification Delivery (email), (4) Subscription Management (view/cancel subscriptions in My Account). This feature enables customers to stay engaged with desired out-of-stock products.

F.finding_title: Missing Feature: Customer Reward Points System
F.business_story: As a customer, I want to earn reward points on my purchases and redeem them for discounts, So that I feel valued and incentivized to continue shopping
F.reasoning: Multiple requirements mention 'reward points' as display elements: REQ004 mentions 'reward points earned' in order totals, REQ047 mentions reward points in order summary, and REQ002 mentions 'reward points' as accessible in 'My Account'. However, there is NO requirement describing the complete reward points feature: (1) How points are earned, (2) Point calculation rules, (3) How to redeem points, (4) Point expiration, (5) Reward tiers. The feature is implied but not explicitly specified.
F.recommendation: Create a new feature 'F30: Loyalty and Reward Points' with sub-features: (1) Reward Points Earning (point per purchase rule), (2) Points Display (current balance in account/checkout), (3) Points Redemption (redeem as discount in checkout), (4) Reward Tier Management (if applicable). This clarifies customer expectations for earning and using rewards, improving satisfaction and retention.

F.finding_title: Validation Requirements Are Feature-Level, Not Implementation Details
F.business_story: As a quality manager, I want validation rules separated from feature definitions so that acceptance criteria focus on business behavior, not implementation mechanics
F.reasoning: Multiple requirements are purely technical validation concerns (REQ055: Email validation, REQ062: Mandatory fields, REQ066: Form validation, REQ067: Email format validation, REQ068: Zip code validation). These are important for quality but shouldn't be standalone 'features'. They're implementation details of larger features (Registration, Address Entry, etc.). Current structure makes the feature list harder to read and prioritize from a business perspective.
F.recommendation: Move validation requirements into acceptance criteria of their parent features rather than standalone requirements. Example: Move REQ067 (Email format validation) into F16 (User Registration) acceptance criteria: 'Email field validates email format before submission'. This keeps feature definitions focused on business value while maintaining validation rigor in acceptance criteria.

F.finding_title: Configuration Settings Mentioned But Not Modeled as Features
F.business_story: As a store administrator, I want to configure system settings and feature behavior through admin interfaces, So that I can customize the platform without code changes
F.reasoning: Requirements extensively reference admin configuration (e.g., 'configurable via Configuration → Settings → Catalog settings'). However, there is NO explicit feature describing the store administration/configuration interface. The requirements assume admin capabilities exist but don't define them as features. From a user perspective, 'Store Configuration' is an important admin feature that should be explicitly modeled.
F.recommendation: Create new features for admin capabilities: 'F31: Store Configuration Interface' with sub-features for each admin area (Catalog Configuration, Order Settings, Payment Configuration, etc.). This makes admin functionality explicit and testable. While less critical for customer-facing features, admin usability significantly impacts operational efficiency.

F.finding_title: Payment Processing Lacks Clear Feature Boundaries
F.business_story: As a payment architect, I want to understand the separation between manual payment entry, payment gateway integration, and payment status management, So that security and compliance requirements are clear
F.reasoning: Payment features are scattered: REQ083-090 cover credit card information entry (manual form), REQ091 mentions secure processing and PCI compliance, REQ101 mentions payment status display. The requirements don't clearly separate: (1) Manual credit card entry (legacy, not recommended), (2) Hosted payment pages (card data never touches merchant server), (3) Payment gateway integration (Stripe, PayPal, etc.), (4) Payment status tracking. This ambiguity creates security risks if developers implement insecure payment capture.
F.recommendation: Restructure payment features: F13 (Payment Information Entry) should explicitly state 'Manual Credit Card Entry' is legacy only and redirect to F13a (Hosted Payment Pages) for production use. Create separate feature: F32 (Payment Gateway Integration) covering Stripe, PayPal, etc. Clarify in F13 requirements that PCI compliance requires hosted payment pages or tokenization, not manual entry. This prevents security vulnerabilities.

F.finding_title: Granularity Inconsistency: Form Fields As Features
F.business_story: As a requirements analyst, I want consistent granularity across all features so that readers understand what constitutes a 'feature' vs. an 'implementation detail'
F.reasoning: Requirements REQ117-142 are extremely granular, treating individual form fields as separate requirements (REQ136: 'Name Input', REQ137: 'Email Input', REQ138: 'Enquiry Input'). This is inconsistent with other areas where entire features (e.g., shopping cart with 9+ actions) are grouped together. This granularity makes it hard to understand feature scope — are name/email inputs really separate features or just form fields within a 'Contact Form' feature?
F.recommendation: Standardize granularity: Features should represent distinct user capabilities/workflows, not form fields. The Contact Form feature (F09a) should be specified at the level of 'Customers can submit inquiries via contact form' with form field specifications in acceptance criteria, not as separate requirements. This makes the feature list easier to navigate and more aligned with user-centric decomposition.

O.outcome_description: The system will send a notification email when a customer abandons their shopping cart
O.triggering_scenario: Customer adds items to cart but does not proceed to checkout within a configurable timeframe
O.reasoning: REQ004 (Shopping Cart Management) defines cart features but does not specify any outcomes for abandoned carts. No recovery mechanism, reminder email, or notification is mentioned. This is a significant gap as cart abandonment is a major e-commerce concern with typical recovery rates improving conversion by 5-10%. Feature F04 (Shopping Cart Management) lacks this critical business outcome.
O.recommendation: Define a specific requirement for cart abandonment recovery: timing (24-48 hours), notification mechanism (email), content (discount incentive options), and tracking of recovery effectiveness. Add to Feature F04 sub-features: 'Abandoned cart recovery email'

O.outcome_description: The system will automatically notify the customer and provide retry options when payment authorization fails
O.triggering_scenario: Payment gateway rejects the transaction (insufficient funds, expired card, etc.)
O.reasoning: Requirements F12-F13 (Payment Method/Information) define the collection and validation of payment details but have no specification of outcomes when payment fails. REQ090 validates card details but doesn't address what happens after authorization failure. No mention of: retry mechanisms, error messages beyond validation, customer notification, order hold, or resumption capability. This is critical for customer experience and recovery of lost sales.
O.recommendation: Add specific requirement: When payment authorization fails, the system shall display detailed error message, offer retry option, save transaction attempt for audit, send failure notification email with recovery instructions, and optionally offer alternative payment methods. Consider implementing: 'Payment Retry' and 'Payment Failure Notification' as new sub-features.

O.finding_title: Null Outcome: Order Confirmation Email Not Explicitly Specified
O.outcome_description: The system will send an order confirmation email to the customer after successful order placement
O.triggering_scenario: Customer completes checkout and confirms order
O.reasoning: While REQ099-REQ107 (Order Details/History) extensively define what information is displayed on order details pages, there is no explicit requirement stating that an order confirmation email SHALL be sent to the customer. The system displays order number, date, status after completion (REQ099) but the outcome of 'system sends confirmation email' is not articulated as a requirement. This is an implicit assumption, not an explicit outcome specification.
O.recommendation: Create explicit requirement: 'The system shall send an order confirmation email containing order number, items, total, and delivery estimate to the customer immediately upon order completion, using the ConfigUrableOrderConfirmed message template.' Add to Feature F14 (Order Confirmation and Review) or create new F101_Order Confirmation Email sub-feature.

O.outcome_description: The system will notify the store administrator when product inventory falls below a critical threshold
O.triggering_scenario: Stock level decreases during order processing or manual inventory adjustments
O.reasoning: Requirements REQ030 (Quantity Selection) and REQ029 (Add to Cart) validate stock availability but have no specification of administrative outcomes. The system checks 'if inventory tracking is enabled' and validates quantities don't exceed stock (REQ040), but there is NO requirement that the system notifies administrators of low stock, out-of-stock events, or backorder status. This gaps the operational visibility needed for inventory management.
O.recommendation: Add requirement: 'The system shall send a low stock alert email to the store administrator when a product's stock quantity falls below a configurable threshold (e.g., 10 units), and shall notify when a product transitions to out-of-stock status.' Define which stakeholders receive notifications, threshold configurations, and alert frequency.

O.outcome_description: The system will send an email confirmation or verification message upon customer registration
O.triggering_scenario: Customer submits the registration form (Feature F16, REQ051, REQ117-REQ125)
O.reasoning: REQ051-REQ059 define the registration form fields, validation rules, and password complexity BUT do not explicitly state the outcome: 'the system sends a confirmation email.' REQ054 mentions 'Email validation required (confirmation email sent)' as a registration METHOD option, not as an outcome of registration itself. The requirement conflates configuration options with outcomes. No explicit requirement states the welcome email, verification email, or account created notification WILL be sent.
O.recommendation: Create explicit outcome requirement: 'When registration is completed, the system shall send a confirmation email to the provided email address containing: account created confirmation, login instructions, account details summary, and a link to activate the account (if email validation is required). The welcome email SHALL be sent using the 'Customer.WelcomeMessage' or 'Customer.EmailValidationMessage' template depending on configuration.' Add as Feature F16 sub-feature or standalone.

O.finding_title: Conflicting Outcome: Guest Checkout Restrictions Undefined
O.outcome_description: The system restrictions on guest checkout for downloadable products are not clearly specified in terms of outcomes
O.triggering_scenario: Customer with downloadable products in cart attempts guest checkout
O.reasoning: REQ050 states 'Guest checkout option is not available if all products in the cart are downloadable and Require registration for downloadable products is enabled.' REQ058 repeats this condition. However, the OUTCOME when this restriction is triggered is not specified: Does the system block checkout with error message? Does it force registration? Does it remove downloadable items? Does it auto-convert to registered user flow? The behavior is ambiguous. Also, REQ003 and F03 specify wishlists require registered users but the outcome when a guest tries to access wishlist is not specified.
O.recommendation: Clarify the outcome: 'When a guest customer with downloadable products attempts checkout and the Require registration setting is enabled, the system SHALL: (1) display a notification message explaining the requirement, (2) block the guest checkout button, (3) offer registration or login as the only path forward.' Create specific requirement for this conditional behavior.

O.finding_title: Missing Outcome: Stock Availability Verification at Order Completion
O.outcome_description: The system will verify that all ordered products remain in stock immediately before order confirmation
O.triggering_scenario: Customer reaches order confirmation step (REQ098) - time lag between adding to cart and confirming order
O.reasoning: REQ098 (Order Verification) states 'stock verification' is performed on the Confirm Order step, but does not specify the outcome IF stock is no longer available. What happens if: a product sold out between cart and confirmation? Quantity reduced? Stock became unavailable for another customer? The requirement validates existence but not the outcome of validation failure. No specification of: error message, suggested actions, order blocking, stock holding mechanism, or customer notification.
O.recommendation: Define outcome: 'If at order confirmation any item is found to be out of stock or insufficient quantity available, the system SHALL: (1) block order confirmation, (2) display which items are unavailable and remaining stock, (3) offer options: remove item, reduce quantity, or return to cart to modify.' Clarify whether stock is reserved during checkout or only validated at confirmation.

O.finding_title: Missing Outcome: Reward Points Redemption and Balance Management
O.outcome_description: The system will update customer reward points balance after order completion and redemption
O.triggering_scenario: Customer completes order (REQ047, REQ093) or redeems reward points
